story:
Two unseen narrators—twins sharing the same cramped, warm world—whisper and bicker in the dark. One rouses the other: Shh. Listen. Beneath the steady shush-shush and thumpity-thump they’ve always known, they hear voices. The light, floating one hums and sways them to sleep; the deep baritone leans close and makes them giggle. They remember being called jelly bean, nugget, peanut. Now the voices toss around grown-up names. The twins joke and poke—ow, stop kicking!—and puzzle things out together. Maybe those voices are choosing names for us. The baritone suggests reusing their own fathers’ names; the light voice wants strong, manly names like their father. So “Father” must be the deep one, they decide. Are we boys? Hard to tell when our eyes aren’t open, they quip, but they assume yes and press closer, feeling for answers they can’t yet see.

Space tightens. The gentle cradle starts to swell and squeeze. A kick isn’t a kick—something else is moving them. The light voice sharpens; the baritone quickens. More voices crowd in, excited and busy, talking about them. Swish, swish, thump, swish—everything rushes. One twin whispers trust me; the other clings to familiar rhythms. Then brightness flares where there’s never been light.

Hands find the first twin. He feels himself lifted, unmoored. The one left behind protests—No! Don’t leave me! We’re two peas in a pod!—but cold bites his brother’s skin and air burns his throat and a cry rips free. The second fights it—get your hands off me, this is my home!—but he, too, bursts into wails as the world opens.

Their duet breaks into two solos. One finds himself in a warm glass box, swaddled, pricked, and taped, with tubes and blinking things peering in. He misses the constant hush and the sleepy partner who always hogged the space. He hears the baritone—Father—close by, saying his twin is right next door in another glass box. They’ve been weighed and measured: tiny for newborns, a little early. He jokes the other was a fatty, always taking more room, and wonders if they’ll see each other again soon.

The other twin, jarred by new sounds and hands, aches for the thumpity-thump and the light voice that hummed them safe. Now there are pokes, prods, oohs and ahhs, and a bright, strange calm. He listens: Father says, Thank you, doctors. A calm reply comes: everything went exceptionally well; there are two healthy boys. Father’s voice warms with pride—identical boys with strong lungs—and softens when he speaks of their mother, sleeping after an early cesarean. Names? They’d been discussing them when the pains started; they haven’t chosen yet.

Separated but side by side, the twins float in their new small worlds, tethered by a steady presence outside the glass. They’re no longer jelly bean or nugget or peanut. They’re something unnamed but certain: brothers who learned each other by shush and thump, by kicks and jokes and shared wonder, who crossed the bright threshold together, crying in harmony, and landed to the sound of a father’s steady baritone promising they are safe, they are loved, and soon, they will have names.
